---
title: "KV Cache: From O(n²) to O(n) in LLM Inference"
description: "A code-first deep dive into the math, memory bottlenecks, and implementation of the KV Cache in autoregressive Transformers."
categories: [Deep Learning, Transformers, Inference, PyTorch, LLMs]
image: "hero_image.png"
draft: false
author: "Ramu Nalla"
date: "2026-04-25"
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    toc-title: "Contents"
---

<div class="blog-manual-meta">Published by Ramu Nalla - April 25, 2026</div>

![KV Cache Latency Comparison](hero_image.png){width=80% fig-align="center"}

---

Scaling language model inference fundamentally comes down to battling two major bottlenecks: **Compute (FLOPs)** and **Memory Bandwidth (VRAM)**. 

When a Transformer generates text, it does so autoregressively—one token at a time. The naive approach to this generation is mathematically elegant but computationally disastrous. If we ask a model to generate the 100th token, the naive approach recalculates the attention scores for tokens 1 through 99 all over again. 

The **KV Cache (Key-Value Cache)** is the industry-standard architectural hack that solves this compute bottleneck. By caching the intermediate mathematical states of past tokens, we reduce the time complexity of generation from a quadratically growing $O(n^2)$ nightmare into a flat, predictable $O(n)$ operation.

In this post, I will walk through the **concept**, the **foundational math**, a **from-scratch PyTorch implementation**, and a deep dive into the **hardware-level profiling** of what actually happens in VRAM when we turn the cache on versus off.

## 1. The Math: Why Autoregressive Generation is Slow

### The Attention Mechanism
In a standard Transformer, the core dot-product attention equation is:

$$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$

During the **Prefill Phase** (processing the initial prompt), the model reads the entire sequence in parallel. The Queries ($Q$), Keys ($K$), and Values ($V$) are all $N \times d$ matrices (where $N$ is sequence length and $d$ is embedding dimension). The $QK^T$ multiplication results in an $N \times N$ attention matrix. 

### The Generation Bottleneck (No Cache)
During the **Decoding Phase**, the model predicts the next token. If we do not use a cache, the input sequence length grows by 1 every step. 

* To generate token 100, we pass an input of size $100$. The model computes a $100 \times 100$ attention matrix.
* To generate token 101, we pass an input of size $101$. The model computes a $101 \times 101$ attention matrix.

The model is redundantly re-computing the Keys and Values for tokens 1 through 100. This results in $O(n^2)$ time complexity. As $N$ grows, the compute required to generate a single word skyrockets.

### The KV Cache Solution
The mathematical realization behind the KV cache is that for a newly generated token, its Query vector only needs to attend to the Key vectors of *past* tokens. The past tokens' Keys and Values do not change. 

If we store (cache) the $K$ and $V$ tensors of all previous tokens in memory:

1. The new input is just the single newly generated token ($1 \times d$).
2. We project this single token into a $1 \times d$ Query, Key, and Value.
3. We append the new $1 \times d$ Key and Value to our cached history.
4. We multiply the $1 \times d$ Query against the $N \times d$ cached Keys. 

The resulting attention matrix is $1 \times N$. The compute time drops to $O(n)$, and latency flattens out entirely.

## 2. Minimal PyTorch Implementation

To see this in action, I built a stripped-down Attention module and a state manager to route the memory.

### The Cache State Manager
This module intercepts the Keys and Values for the newly generated token and forcefully concatenates them to the existing history.

```python
import torch
from typing import Tuple, Optional

class StandardKVCache:
    """
    Implements a standard, contiguous KV-Cache.
    Tensors grow linearly with each generation step.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self.k_cache: Optional[torch.Tensor] = None
        self.v_cache: Optional[torch.Tensor] = None

    def update(self, k_new: torch.Tensor, v_new: torch.Tensor) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
        """Appends new keys and values to the contiguous cache."""
        if self.k_cache is None or self.v_cache is None:
            self.k_cache = k_new
            self.v_cache = v_new
        else:
            # Concatenate along the sequence length dimension (dim=2)
            self.k_cache = torch.cat([self.k_cache, k_new], dim=2)
            self.v_cache = torch.cat([self.v_cache, v_new], dim=2)
            
        return self.k_cache, self.v_cache

    def reset(self):
        self.k_cache = None
        self.v_cache = None
```

### The Model Backbone
This implements the core attention math, conditionally routing it through the cache. Notice the torch.cat bottleneck under the hood.

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import math

class MinimalAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, embed_dim=256, num_heads=8):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.head_dim = embed_dim // num_heads
        
        self.q_proj = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.k_proj = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.v_proj = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.out_proj = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor, kv_cache: StandardKVCache = None) -> torch.Tensor:
        batch_size, seq_len, embed_dim = x.shape
        
        # 1. Project Q, K, V
        q = self.q_proj(x).view(batch_size, seq_len, self.num_heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.k_proj(x).view(batch_size, seq_len, self.num_heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.v_proj(x).view(batch_size, seq_len, self.num_heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        # 2. KV-Cache Routing
        if kv_cache is not None:
            # Standard Cache active: append new K, V and fetch full history
            k, v = kv_cache.update(k, v)
        
        # 3. Core Attention Math
        scores = torch.matmul(q, k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(self.head_dim)
        
        if kv_cache is None and seq_len > 1:
            mask = torch.tril(torch.ones(seq_len, seq_len, device=x.device)).view(1, 1, seq_len, seq_len)
            scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float('-inf'))
            
        attn_weights = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
        out = torch.matmul(attn_weights, v)
        
        # 4. Reassemble and project
        out = out.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(batch_size, seq_len, embed_dim)
        return self.out_proj(out)
```

### The Profiling Harness
To prove the math, we run a 1000-step autoregressive loop, tracking the Time-Per-Output-Token (TPOT).

```python
import torch
import time
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def simulate_inference(attention_layer, prefix_len, gen_steps, use_cache=False, device='cpu'):
    embed_dim = 256
    latencies = []
    
    # Context Phase (Prefill)
    prefix_tokens = torch.randn(1, prefix_len, embed_dim, device=device)
    cache = StandardKVCache() if use_cache else None
    
    start_time = time.perf_counter()
    _ = attention_layer(prefix_tokens, kv_cache=cache)
    ttft = time.perf_counter() - start_time
    
    # Generation Phase
    current_seq_len = prefix_len
    
    for _ in range(gen_steps):
        step_start = time.perf_counter()
        
        if use_cache:
            next_token = torch.randn(1, 1, embed_dim, device=device)
            _ = attention_layer(next_token, kv_cache=cache)
        else:
            current_seq_len += 1
            full_sequence = torch.randn(1, current_seq_len, embed_dim, device=device)
            _ = attention_layer(full_sequence, kv_cache=None)
            
        step_time = time.perf_counter() - step_start
        latencies.append(step_time * 1000)
        
    return ttft * 1000, latencies

# ... (Execution wrapper omitted for brevity) ...
```

## 3. Hardware Realities: Reading the Profiler Plot

When we execute the above code on hardware (in this case, an Apple Silicon Mac via the MPS backend), the resulting latency plot reveals a story that is much messier than the theoretical $O(n^2)$ math.

![](stage1_baseline_latency.png){width=70% fig-align="center"}

**Aggregate timings from the run** (prefix prefill for TTFT, then 1000 decode steps; same harness as the code above, MPS on Apple Silicon):

::: {.blog-content-table}

|  | TTFT (ms) | Avg TPOT (ms) |
|:--|----------:|-------------:|
| **No cache** | 220.12 | 65.29 |
| **With cache** | 5.11 | 6.20 |

:::

* **Average TPOT (time per output token)** is the average time to produce one new token during generation. Here: **65.29 ms** without a cache versus **6.20 ms** with it—about **10×** faster with the cache, because each step reuses stored keys and values instead of recomputing attention over the full sequence so far.

* **TTFT (time to first token)** is how long the initial prefill takes before the first token is ready. Here: **220.12 ms** without a cache versus **5.11 ms** with it—about **43×** faster with the cache, because the cached path does not pay the same one-shot cost before decode can start.

### The "Memory Quake" (The 7000ms Spike)

Around step 520, the No Cache (red) line hits a massive 7-second latency wall. Why? Because the bottleneck here isn't actually the FLOPs required to multiply the matrices—it is PyTorch's Caching Memory Allocator.

In the "No Cache" loop, we ask the hardware to calculate a continuously growing $N \times N$ attention grid. At step 520, the operating system ran out of readily available contiguous VRAM. It had to "stop the world," trigger garbage collection, defragment the memory, and allocate a massive new contiguous block before inference could resume.

### The Post-Spike Drop

Notice how the red line's baseline latency actually drops and flattens out after the 7000ms spike. Because PyTorch successfully negotiated a massive, fresh block of VRAM during that pause, the subsequent hundreds of steps no longer suffer from constant OS-level memory allocation overhead. The latency becomes purely compute-bound, proving that in LLM inference, memory management often dictates speed more than raw compute capability.

### The Flatline of the KV Cache

The blue line (Standard KV-Cache) bypasses the $N \times N$ grid entirely, maintaining a rock-solid $\sim6\text{ms}$ latency. The memory allocator handles the tiny $1 \times N$ requests effortlessly.

## Conclusion

The KV Cache perfectly solves the compute scaling problem of autoregressive generation. However, it is not a silver bullet. As we saw in our code, the StandardKVCache relies on torch.cat, meaning it continuously requests linearly larger blocks of contiguous memory.

While it solves the compute bottleneck, the standard KV cache introduces a massive memory capacity bottleneck: VRAM fragmentation. Pre-allocating contiguous VRAM for maximum sequence lengths wastes incredible amounts of memory, which is exactly why modern inference engines have abandoned this approach in favor of advanced OS-style block memory management (PagedAttention).
